fact: NASA was established by President Dwight D. Eisenhower, creating America's civilian space agency to lead the nation's space exploration efforts
Explorer 1, America's first artificial satellite, was launched from Cape Canaveral, discovering the Van Allen radiation belts surrounding Earth
Pioneer 0, NASA's first lunar probe attempt, was destroyed 77 seconds after launch due to a Thor rocket failure
Pioneer 1, the first spacecraft launched by NASA, achieved the highest altitude of any human-made object at the time (113,854 km) but failed to reach the Moon
Pioneer 4, the first American spacecraft to escape Earth's gravity, successfully flew by the Moon at a distance of 60,000 km
The Mercury Seven astronauts were introduced to the American public as NASA's first class of astronauts, including Alan Shepard, John Glenn, and Gus Grissom
Mercury-Redstone 3 (Freedom 7) launched Alan Shepard as the first American in space on a 15-minute suborbital flight reaching 187 km altitude
President John F. Kennedy announced the goal of landing a man on the Moon before the end of the decade in his historic address to Congress
Mercury-Redstone 4 (Liberty Bell 7) launched Virgil 'Gus' Grissom on America's second crewed suborbital spaceflight; the capsule sank after splashdown
Mercury-Atlas 6 (Friendship 7) made John Glenn the first American to orbit Earth, completing three orbits in 4 hours and 55 minutes
Mercury-Atlas 7 (Aurora 7) launched Scott Carpenter on America's second orbital flight, completing three orbits around Earth
Mercury-Atlas 8 (Sigma 7) launched Walter Schirra on a six-orbit mission, demonstrating precision spacecraft control
Mercury-Atlas 9 (Faith 7) launched Gordon Cooper on the final Mercury mission, completing 22 orbits in 34 hours
Mariner 2 became the first spacecraft to successfully fly by another planet, passing within 34,773 km of Venus
Gemini 3 launched astronauts Gus Grissom and John Young on the first crewed Gemini mission, nicknamed 'Molly Brown'
Gemini 4 astronaut Ed White became the first American to walk in space during a 23-minute EVA, using a hand-held maneuvering unit
Gemini 5 launched astronauts Gordon Cooper and Pete Conrad on an 8-day mission, setting a new spaceflight duration record
Gemini 6A and Gemini 7 performed the first crewed spacecraft rendezvous, approaching within 30 cm of each other in orbit
Gemini 7 launched astronauts Frank Borman and Jim Lovell on a 14-day mission, demonstrating humans could survive in space long enough for a lunar mission
Gemini 8 astronauts Neil Armstrong and David Scott performed the first docking of two spacecraft in orbit, connecting with an Agena target vehicle
Gemini 9A astronaut Gene Cernan performed a 2-hour EVA, though difficulties with his spacesuit caused exhaustion and visor fogging
Gemini 10 astronauts John Young and Michael Collins used the Agena engine to reach a record altitude of 763 km
Gemini 11 astronauts Pete Conrad and Richard Gordon reached a record altitude of 1,374 km using the Agena target vehicle
Gemini 12 completed the Gemini program with astronauts Jim Lovell and Buzz Aldrin, who performed three successful EVAs
The Apollo 1 fire killed astronauts Gus Grissom, Ed White, and Roger Chaffee during a launch pad test at Cape Kennedy
Apollo 4 successfully tested the Saturn V rocket for the first time, reaching an altitude of 18,000 km in an uncrewed mission
Apollo 7 launched astronauts Walter Schirra, Donn Eisele, and Walter Cunningham on the first crewed Apollo mission, an 11-day Earth orbital flight
Apollo 8 launched astronauts Frank Borman, Jim Lovell, and William Anders on the first crewed mission to orbit the Moon
Apollo 8 astronauts became the first humans to see the far side of the Moon and captured the iconic 'Earthrise' photograph
Apollo 9 launched to test the Lunar Module in Earth orbit, with astronauts James McDivitt, David Scott, and Rusty Schweickart
Apollo 10 launched astronauts Tom Stafford, John Young, and Gene Cernan on a 'dress rehearsal' for the Moon landing, descending to within 15 km of the lunar surface
Apollo 11 launched from Kennedy Space Center, beginning humanity's first mission to land on the Moon with astronauts Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins
Apollo 11's Lunar Module Eagle landed in the Sea of Tranquility, making the first crewed Moon landing with Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin aboard
Buzz Aldrin joined Neil Armstrong on the lunar surface 19 minutes after Armstrong's first step, becoming the second human to walk on the Moon
Apollo 11 astronauts planted the American flag on the Moon and deployed scientific instruments during their 2.5-hour moonwalk
Apollo 11 splashed down safely in the Pacific Ocean, completing the historic first Moon landing mission
Apollo 12 launched astronauts Pete Conrad, Richard Gordon, and Alan Bean on the second Moon landing mission, demonstrating precision landing capability
Apollo 12's Lunar Module Intrepid landed within walking distance of Surveyor 3, allowing astronauts to retrieve parts from the probe
Pete Conrad, an Ohio native, became the third human to walk on the Moon and quipped 'Whoopee! Man, that may have been a small one for Neil, but that's a long one for me!'
Apollo 13 launched with astronauts Jim Lovell (from Cleveland, Ohio), Jack Swigert, and Fred Haise on what became NASA's most famous 'successful failure'
An oxygen tank explosion crippled Apollo 13's Service Module, forcing the crew to abort their Moon landing and use the Lunar Module as a lifeboat
Apollo 13 splashed down safely in the Pacific Ocean after a harrowing 4-day journey home, demonstrating NASA's crisis management capabilities
Apollo 14 launched with astronauts Alan Shepard, Stuart Roosa, and Edgar Mitchell, returning America to the Moon after the Apollo 13 incident
Alan Shepard became the oldest person to walk on the Moon at age 47 and famously hit golf balls on the lunar surface during Apollo 14
Apollo 15 launched with astronauts David Scott, Alfred Worden, and James Irwin, becoming the first mission to use the Lunar Roving Vehicle
Apollo 15 astronaut David Scott performed the famous 'hammer and feather' demonstration on the Moon, confirming Galileo's theory that objects fall at the same rate in a vacuum
Apollo 16 launched with astronauts John Young, Ken Mattingly, and Charles Duke to explore the lunar highlands at Descartes
Apollo 17 launched as the final Apollo lunar mission, with astronauts Eugene Cernan, Ronald Evans, and Harrison Schmitt
Apollo 17 was the only night launch of the Apollo program, creating a spectacular display visible for hundreds of miles
Gene Cernan (born in Chicago, Illinois) became the last human to walk on the Moon during Apollo 17, leaving his final footprints on December 14, 1972
Harrison Schmitt became the only professional geologist to walk on the Moon during Apollo 17, discovering orange lunar soil at Shorty Crater
Apollo 17 astronauts captured the famous 'Blue Marble' photograph of Earth, one of the most reproduced images in history
Pioneer 10 launched from Cape Canaveral, becoming the first spacecraft designed to travel through the asteroid belt and study Jupiter
Pioneer 11 launched to explore Jupiter and Saturn, carrying a golden plaque depicting humanity's origin for potential extraterrestrial discovery
Skylab, America's first space station, was launched on a Saturn V rocket into Earth orbit
Skylab 2 (SL-2) launched with astronauts Pete Conrad, Joseph Kerwin, and Paul Weitz to repair and occupy the damaged Skylab station
Skylab 3 (SL-3) launched with astronauts Alan Bean, Owen Garriott, and Jack Lousma for a 59-day mission on America's first space station
Skylab 4 (SL-4) launched with astronauts Gerald Carr, Edward Gibson, and William Pogue on an 84-day mission, the longest American spaceflight at the time
Pioneer 10 became the first spacecraft to fly through the asteroid belt and the first to make a close flyby of Jupiter
Mariner 10 became the first spacecraft to visit Mercury, using a gravity assist from Venus - the first planetary gravity assist maneuver
Pioneer 11 made the first flyby of Saturn, discovering two new moons and a new ring, and measuring the planet's magnetic field
The Apollo-Soyuz Test Project launched, marking the first joint US-Soviet space mission and historic handshake in orbit between astronauts and cosmonauts
Soyuz 19 launched from Baikonur Cosmodrome with cosmonauts Alexei Leonov and Valery Kubasov for the historic Apollo-Soyuz mission
The Apollo and Soyuz spacecraft docked in orbit, and American astronaut Tom Stafford shook hands with Soviet cosmonaut Alexei Leonov, symbolizing détente
Apollo-Soyuz mission commander Tom Stafford became the first general officer to fly in space during the historic joint mission
Deke Slayton finally flew in space on Apollo-Soyuz, 16 years after being selected as one of the original Mercury Seven astronauts
Viking 1 launched from Cape Canaveral to become the first successful Mars lander, carrying instruments to search for life
Viking 1 became the first spacecraft to successfully land on Mars and return images from the surface
Viking 2 landed on Mars at Utopia Planitia, joining Viking 1 in the search for Martian life
Voyager 2 launched from Cape Canaveral, beginning its 'Grand Tour' of the outer solar system that would visit all four giant planets
Voyager 1 launched from Cape Canaveral, 16 days after Voyager 2, on a faster trajectory to Jupiter and Saturn
Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Jupiter, discovering volcanic activity on Io - the first active volcanoes found beyond Earth
Voyager 2 made its closest approach to Jupiter, capturing detailed images of the Great Red Spot and Jupiter's moons
Skylab reentered Earth's atmosphere over Western Australia after NASA could not boost it to a higher orbit
Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Saturn, discovering complex ring structure and new moons
Space Shuttle Columbia (STS-1) launched from Kennedy Space Center with astronauts John Young and Robert Crippen, beginning the Space Shuttle era
STS-1 marked the first crewed maiden voyage of a new spacecraft system in NASA history, with no prior uncrewed orbital test flights
Space Shuttle Columbia landed at Edwards Air Force Base, completing the first Space Shuttle mission after 54.5 hours in orbit
Voyager 2 made its closest approach to Saturn, imaging the planet's rings and moons before continuing to Uranus and Neptune
STS-2 launched Space Shuttle Columbia on its second mission, making it the first reusable spacecraft to fly into orbit twice
STS-5 launched the first commercial satellites from the Space Shuttle, beginning a new era in space commerce
STS-6 launched Space Shuttle Challenger on its maiden flight, deploying the first Tracking and Data Relay Satellite
Sally Ride became the first American woman in space aboard Space Shuttle Challenger during mission STS-7
Guion Bluford became the first African American in space aboard Space Shuttle Challenger during mission STS-8
STS-9 launched with the first Spacelab module, a reusable laboratory built by the European Space Agency
Bruce McCandless performed the first untethered spacewalk using the Manned Maneuvering Unit during STS-41-B, flying up to 98 meters from the shuttle
STS-41-C successfully captured and repaired the Solar Maximum Mission satellite, demonstrating in-orbit satellite servicing
STS-41-D launched Space Shuttle Discovery on its maiden flight, the third operational orbiter in the Space Shuttle fleet
Kathryn Sullivan became the first American woman to walk in space during STS-41-G
STS-51-A astronauts retrieved two malfunctioning communications satellites in orbit and returned them to Earth in the shuttle's cargo bay
STS-51-C launched Space Shuttle Discovery on the first dedicated Department of Defense Space Shuttle mission
STS-51-D launched Senator Jake Garn, the first sitting member of Congress to fly in space
STS-51-J launched Space Shuttle Atlantis on its maiden flight, the fourth operational Space Shuttle orbiter
STS-61-B deployed the largest structure ever assembled in space at the time - the EASE/ACCESS experimental tower
Space Shuttle Challenger (STS-51-L) broke apart 73 seconds after launch, killing all seven crew members including teacher Christa McAuliffe
The Challenger disaster was caused by O-ring failure in the right solid rocket booster, leading to structural breakup at 48,000 feet
Christa McAuliffe, selected as the first Teacher in Space, was among the seven Challenger crew members lost in the disaster
Voyager 2 made the first and only flyby of Uranus, discovering 10 new moons and two new rings
STS-26 launched Space Shuttle Discovery, marking NASA's return to flight 32 months after the Challenger disaster
Voyager 2 made the first and only flyby of Neptune, discovering the Great Dark Spot and six new moons
Voyager 2's Neptune flyby revealed Triton as the coldest object yet measured in the solar system at -235°C
Space Shuttle Atlantis deployed the Magellan spacecraft to Venus during STS-30, the first planetary probe launched from a Space Shuttle
Space Shuttle Atlantis deployed the Galileo spacecraft to Jupiter during STS-34
The Hubble Space Telescope was deployed from Space Shuttle Discovery during STS-31, beginning a revolution in astronomy
Hubble Space Telescope was placed into orbit 340 miles above Earth, designed to operate for 15 years but still functioning decades later
The Magellan spacecraft began mapping Venus with radar, eventually mapping 98% of the planet's surface in unprecedented detail
STS-37 deployed the Compton Gamma Ray Observatory, the heaviest satellite ever launched by the Space Shuttle at the time
The Galileo spacecraft flew by asteroid Gaspra, returning the first close-up images of an asteroid
Mae Jemison became the first African American woman in space during STS-47
STS-49 launched Space Shuttle Endeavour on its maiden flight, the newest and final orbiter built to replace Challenger
STS-49 astronauts performed the first three-person spacewalk to capture the stranded Intelsat VI satellite
STS-61 successfully repaired the Hubble Space Telescope's flawed mirror, restoring its ability to produce sharp images
The Hubble servicing mission STS-61 featured five spacewalks over 11 days, one of the most complex shuttle missions ever
Clementine became the first US spacecraft to return to the Moon since Apollo 17, mapping the lunar surface and discovering water ice evidence
STS-63 featured the first female Space Shuttle pilot, Eileen Collins, and the first close approach to the Russian Mir space station
Norman Thagard became the first American astronaut to fly on a Russian spacecraft, launching aboard Soyuz TM-21 to Mir
STS-71 became the first Space Shuttle to dock with the Russian Mir space station, beginning the Shuttle-Mir program
The Galileo spacecraft entered orbit around Jupiter, becoming the first spacecraft to orbit an outer planet
The Galileo atmospheric probe descended into Jupiter's atmosphere, transmitting data for 57 minutes before being crushed
Shannon Lucid began her 188-day stay aboard Mir, setting the American and world record for a woman's longest spaceflight
Mars Pathfinder launched from Cape Canaveral, carrying the Sojourner rover - the first wheeled vehicle on Mars
Mars Global Surveyor launched to begin detailed mapping of Mars, eventually operating for nearly 10 years
Mars Pathfinder landed on Mars and deployed the Sojourner rover, the first successful Mars landing since Viking in 1976
Sojourner became the first wheeled vehicle to operate on another planet, exploring Mars for 83 days
The Cassini-Huygens spacecraft launched toward Saturn on a 7-year journey to study the ringed planet and its moons
STS-88 launched the first US component of the International Space Station, the Unity connecting module
John Glenn returned to space at age 77 aboard STS-95, becoming the oldest person to fly in space, 36 years after his Mercury mission
John Glenn, born in Cambridge, Ohio, circled Earth 134 times during STS-95 compared to just 3 orbits on his historic Mercury flight
Deep Space 1 launched to test ion propulsion and other advanced technologies, later flying by asteroid Braille and comet Borrelly
Mars Polar Lander launched to study the Martian south pole, but was lost during landing due to a software error
Eileen Collins became the first female Space Shuttle commander during STS-93, which deployed the Chandra X-ray Observatory
The Chandra X-ray Observatory was deployed from Space Shuttle Columbia, becoming the world's most powerful X-ray telescope
NASA's Mars Climate Orbiter was lost due to a navigation error caused by mixing metric and imperial units
The International Space Station received its first permanent crew when Expedition 1 (Bill Shepherd, Yuri Gidzenko, Sergei Krikalev) arrived aboard Soyuz TM-31
STS-92 delivered the Z1 truss segment to the ISS, beginning assembly of the station's backbone structure
The NEAR Shoemaker spacecraft became the first to orbit an asteroid, entering orbit around 433 Eros
NEAR Shoemaker made the first controlled landing on an asteroid, touching down on Eros and transmitting for two weeks
The Destiny laboratory module was attached to the ISS during STS-98, becoming the primary research facility for the US segment
Mars Odyssey launched to study Mars' geology and search for water ice, becoming the longest-serving spacecraft at Mars
Dennis Tito became the first space tourist, paying $20 million to fly to the ISS aboard Soyuz TM-32
Genesis launched to collect samples of solar wind particles and return them to Earth
Space Shuttle Columbia (STS-107) launched on a 16-day science mission that would end in tragedy during reentry
Space Shuttle Columbia broke apart during reentry over Texas and Louisiana, killing all seven crew members including Ilan Ramon, Israel's first astronaut
The Columbia disaster was caused by foam insulation striking the wing's leading edge during launch, allowing hot gases to penetrate during reentry
Columbia commander Rick Husband's last words from space were 'Roger, uh...' before the signal was lost
Spirit (MER-A) launched toward Mars, beginning the remarkably successful Mars Exploration Rover mission
Opportunity (MER-B) launched toward Mars, destined to far exceed its 90-day design life
Spitzer Space Telescope launched, becoming NASA's Great Observatory for infrared astronomy
Spirit rover landed on Mars in Gusev Crater, designed for a 90-day mission but operating for over 6 years
Opportunity rover landed on Mars at Meridiani Planum, eventually traveling over 45 km and operating for nearly 15 years
The Cassini spacecraft entered orbit around Saturn after a 7-year journey, beginning a 13-year mission studying the ringed planet
Genesis sample return capsule crashed in the Utah desert when its parachutes failed to deploy, but scientists still recovered solar wind samples
SpaceShipOne won the $10 million Ansari X Prize by becoming the first private crewed spacecraft to reach space twice within two weeks
The Huygens probe separated from Cassini to begin its descent to Saturn's moon Titan
The Huygens probe landed on Titan, becoming the first landing in the outer solar system and returning the first images of Titan's surface
Deep Impact successfully slammed an impactor into Comet Tempel 1, creating a crater and ejecting material for study
STS-114 launched Space Shuttle Discovery on NASA's return-to-flight mission after the Columbia disaster
Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter launched to study Mars from orbit with the most powerful camera ever sent to another planet
New Horizons launched toward Pluto, beginning a 9-year journey to the dwarf planet and the Kuiper Belt
New Horizons became the fastest spacecraft ever launched, leaving Earth at 36,373 mph (58,536 km/h)
Stardust returned samples from Comet Wild 2 to Earth, the first comet samples ever brought back for study
STS-121 launched Space Shuttle Discovery, the second return-to-flight mission after Columbia, testing new safety procedures
Phoenix Mars Lander launched to study the Martian arctic and search for water ice
Dawn spacecraft launched to visit asteroids Vesta and Ceres, using ion propulsion to visit two different worlds
Phoenix became the first spacecraft to land in Mars' polar region, confirming the presence of water ice just below the surface
Phoenix directly observed and photographed water ice on Mars, watching it sublimate over several days
The Hubble Space Telescope was serviced for the final time during STS-125, installing new instruments to extend its life
Kepler Space Telescope launched to search for Earth-like planets around other stars
LCROSS impacted the Moon's Cabeus crater, confirming the presence of water ice in permanently shadowed regions
SpaceX Falcon 9 successfully launched for the first time, beginning a new era in commercial spaceflight
Spirit rover sent its last communication to Earth after becoming stuck in soft soil, ending a mission that lasted over 6 years
Dawn spacecraft entered orbit around asteroid Vesta, becoming the first spacecraft to orbit a main-belt asteroid
Space Shuttle Atlantis (STS-135) launched on the final Space Shuttle mission after 30 years of the program
Space Shuttle Atlantis landed at Kennedy Space Center, ending the 30-year Space Shuttle program with mission STS-135
The Space Shuttle program completed 135 missions over 30 years, carrying 355 different people to space
Curiosity rover (Mars Science Laboratory) launched toward Mars, carrying the most sophisticated science instruments ever sent to the Red Planet
Juno spacecraft launched toward Jupiter to study the gas giant's atmosphere, magnetic field, and interior
Voyager 1 entered interstellar space, becoming the first human-made object to leave the heliosphere
Curiosity rover landed on Mars using the innovative 'sky crane' maneuver, beginning the most ambitious Mars surface mission yet
SpaceX Dragon became the first commercial spacecraft to dock with the International Space Station
Dawn spacecraft left Vesta orbit and began its journey to dwarf planet Ceres
LADEE launched to study the Moon's thin atmosphere and the mysterious lunar dust environment
MAVEN launched to study Mars' upper atmosphere and understand how the planet lost its early atmosphere
NASA's Kepler mission confirmed its 1,000th exoplanet discovery
Dawn spacecraft arrived at dwarf planet Ceres, becoming the first mission to orbit a dwarf planet
New Horizons made the first close flyby of Pluto, revealing a geologically active world with mountains of water ice
New Horizons captured the iconic heart-shaped Tombaugh Regio on Pluto, named after Pluto's discoverer Clyde Tombaugh
SpaceX Falcon 9 successfully landed its first stage back on land at Cape Canaveral, revolutionizing rocket reusability
Scott Kelly returned from the ISS after 340 consecutive days in space, setting the US record for longest single spaceflight
Juno spacecraft entered orbit around Jupiter, beginning its study of the giant planet's deep atmosphere and interior
OSIRIS-REx launched to asteroid Bennu to collect samples and return them to Earth
SpaceX Falcon 9 successfully landed on a drone ship at sea for the first time
Cassini began its 'Grand Finale' orbits, diving between Saturn and its rings for the first time
Cassini plunged into Saturn's atmosphere, ending its 13-year mission with a final blaze of data transmission
Parker Solar Probe launched to become the first spacecraft to 'touch' the Sun, eventually flying through the solar corona
InSight lander touched down on Mars to study the planet's interior using a seismometer and heat probe
Opportunity rover's mission was declared complete after a global dust storm prevented it from recharging its batteries
Opportunity had traveled over 45 km on Mars, setting the all-time record for off-Earth driving distance
New Horizons flew past Arrokoth (2014 MU69), the most distant object ever explored by a spacecraft
SpaceX Crew Dragon Demo-1 launched, becoming the first American spacecraft to autonomously dock with the ISS
Christina Koch set the record for longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days aboard the ISS
SpaceX Crew Dragon Demo-2 launched astronauts Doug Hurley and Bob Behnken, restoring American crewed launch capability
Demo-2 was the first crewed orbital spaceflight from American soil since STS-135 in 2011
Perseverance rover launched to Mars carrying the Ingenuity helicopter for the first powered flight on another planet
OSIRIS-REx successfully collected samples from asteroid Bennu using a touch-and-go maneuver
SpaceX Crew-1 launched with four astronauts on the first operational crew rotation mission to the ISS
Perseverance rover landed on Mars in Jezero Crater, carrying the first helicopter to fly on another planet
Perseverance used the sky crane landing system successfully, touching down in an ancient river delta on Mars
Ingenuity's first Mars flight lasted 39.1 seconds, reaching an altitude of 3 meters, proving flight is possible in Mars' thin atmosphere
The James Webb Space Telescope launched from French Guiana on an Ariane 5 rocket, beginning its journey to the L2 point
JWST is the largest and most powerful space telescope ever built, with a 6.5-meter primary mirror made of gold-plated beryllium
James Webb Space Telescope successfully deployed its sunshield over five days after launch
JWST arrived at its orbit around the L2 Lagrange point, 1.5 million km from Earth
The first full-color images from the James Webb Space Telescope were released, including the deepest infrared view of the universe
DART spacecraft successfully impacted asteroid Dimorphos, demonstrating the first planetary defense technique
DART changed Dimorphos' orbit by 32 minutes, proving asteroids can be deflected by kinetic impact
Artemis I launched on the SLS rocket, sending an uncrewed Orion capsule around the Moon to test systems for future crewed missions
Artemis I set the record for the farthest distance from Earth by a spacecraft designed to carry humans, reaching 432,210 km
Artemis I Orion capsule splashed down in the Pacific Ocean after a successful 25-day mission around the Moon
OSIRIS-REx sample return capsule landed in Utah carrying material from asteroid Bennu
Ingenuity helicopter completed its 72nd and final flight on Mars after nearly 3 years of operations
Neil Armstrong, born in Wapakoneta, Ohio, became the most famous Midwesterner in space history as the first person on the Moon
John Glenn, from Cambridge, Ohio, became the first American to orbit Earth and later the oldest person to fly in space
Jim Lovell, born in Cleveland, Ohio, commanded the ill-fated Apollo 13 mission and was the first person to fly to the Moon twice
Gus Grissom, from Mitchell, Indiana, was the second American in space and commanded the first Gemini mission before dying in the Apollo 1 fire
Judith Resnik, from Akron, Ohio, was the second American woman in space and was killed in the Challenger disaster
Sunita Williams, whose family is from Ohio, holds the record for most spacewalk time by a female astronaut
The Johnson Space Center (JSC) was established in Houston, Texas, becoming NASA's center for human spaceflight
Kennedy Space Center was established as NASA's primary launch facility for crewed missions
The Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL), operated by Caltech for NASA, manages most of NASA's robotic space missions
Goddard Space Flight Center was NASA's first space flight center, named after rocket pioneer Robert Goddard
Telstar 1, the first active communications satellite, transmitted the first live transatlantic television signal
Landsat 1 launched as the first Earth-observing satellite, beginning continuous monitoring of Earth's surface
The first GPS satellite (Navstar 1) was launched, beginning the Global Positioning System constellation
Pioneer 10 crossed Neptune's orbit, becoming the first human-made object to travel beyond the major planets
Pioneer 10's final signal was received by NASA's Deep Space Network before contact was lost forever
Voyager 1 is the farthest human-made object from Earth, traveling over 24 billion km into interstellar space
The Hubble Space Telescope has made over 1.5 million observations since its launch in 1990
X-15 rocket plane set the aircraft speed record of Mach 6.7 (7,274 km/h), piloted by Pete Knight
X-15 pilot Joseph Walker reached an altitude of 107.8 km, exceeding the international definition of space
The X-15 program completed 199 flights and contributed data essential for developing the Space Shuttle
STS-27 Atlantis suffered severe tile damage during launch, coming close to a Columbia-like disaster but surviving reentry
STS-51-L (Challenger) crew members included Francis Scobee, Michael Smith, Judith Resnik, Ellison Onizuka, Ronald McNair, Gregory Jarvis, and Christa McAuliffe
STS-107 (Columbia) crew members included Rick Husband, William McCool, Michael Anderson, Kalpana Chawla, David Brown, Laurel Clark, and Ilan Ramon
Kalpana Chawla was the first woman of Indian origin in space, flying on STS-87 before her fatal STS-107 mission
Peggy Whitson holds the US record for total time in space with 665 days across multiple missions
Frank Rubio set a new US single-spaceflight record of 371 days aboard the ISS
Blue Origin's New Shepard made its first crewed flight with Jeff Bezos aboard, launching from West Texas
Boeing Starliner completed its first crewed test flight to the ISS with astronauts Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams
SpaceX Starship completed its first successful flight and landing during its fourth test flight
SpaceX became the first private company to launch humans to orbit and to the ISS with Crew Dragon
Axiom-1 became the first all-private crewed mission to the ISS, commanded by former astronaut Michael López-Alegría
Inspiration4 was the first all-civilian orbital spaceflight, with no professional astronauts aboard
The Apollo lunar samples total 382 kg of Moon rocks and soil brought back to Earth across six missions
Mariner 4 returned the first close-up images of Mars, revealing a cratered surface unlike Earth
Mariner 9 became the first spacecraft to orbit another planet, mapping the entire surface of Mars
The Saturn V remains the most powerful rocket ever successfully launched, generating 7.5 million pounds of thrust
STS-41-G was the first spaceflight to include two women: Sally Ride and Kathryn Sullivan
Jessica Watkins became the first Black woman to serve on a long-duration ISS mission during Crew-4
The ISS has been continuously occupied since November 2000, marking over 20 years of human presence in space
STS-95 was watched by an estimated 250 million viewers, the largest TV audience for a shuttle mission since Challenger
The Space Shuttle traveled a total of over 870 million km across all 135 missions
President Kennedy's Moon speech at Rice University declared 'We choose to go to the Moon in this decade and do the other things, not because they are easy, but because they are hard'
The Apollo Guidance Computer had less processing power than a modern calculator but successfully navigated humans to the Moon
Apollo 11 astronauts were quarantined for 21 days after returning from the Moon to ensure they hadn't brought back lunar pathogens
Michael Collins orbited the Moon alone during Apollo 11, becoming the most isolated human in history - 3,200 km from the nearest humans
Alan Bean was the fourth person to walk on the Moon and later became a renowned artist painting lunar scenes
Stuart Roosa flew to the Moon on Apollo 14 with hundreds of tree seeds that were later planted across the US as 'Moon Trees'
John Young walked on the Moon during Apollo 16 and later commanded the first Space Shuttle mission, spanning NASA's greatest eras
The Lunar Laser Ranging Experiment retroreflectors left by Apollo astronauts are still used today to measure Earth-Moon distance
Mercury spacecraft were so small that astronaut John Glenn had trouble fitting inside at 5'10" and 180 pounds
Gordon Cooper's Mercury-Atlas 9 mission was the first American spaceflight to last more than a day
Wally Schirra is the only astronaut to fly Mercury, Gemini, and Apollo spacecraft, participating in all three programs
The Space Shuttle main engines were the most efficient rocket engines ever built, with 452 seconds of specific impulse
STS-114 introduced the Shuttle's External Tank inspection boom after the Columbia disaster investigation
Space Shuttle Discovery flew more missions (39) than any other orbiter in the fleet
Space Shuttle Endeavour was built using structural spare parts from Discovery and Atlantis after Challenger was lost
The Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center is so large that clouds can form inside on humid days
Enterprise was the first Space Shuttle orbiter, built for atmospheric test flights but never flew in space
The Space Shuttle's thermal protection system contained over 24,000 individual tiles, each uniquely shaped
TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) launched to search for exoplanets around the brightest stars near Earth
Lucy spacecraft launched to study Jupiter's Trojan asteroids, visiting more asteroids than any previous mission
Europa Clipper launched to study Jupiter's moon Europa and its potential subsurface ocean
Psyche spacecraft launched to study the metal asteroid 16 Psyche, thought to be the core of a protoplanet
Voyager 2 is the only spacecraft to have visited all four giant outer planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune
The Galileo mission discovered evidence of a subsurface ocean on Jupiter's moon Europa, making it a prime target for astrobiology
Cassini discovered water geysers erupting from Saturn's moon Enceladus, suggesting a subsurface ocean
The Mars helicopter Ingenuity far exceeded its planned 5 flights, completing 72 flights over nearly 3 years
Curiosity rover discovered organic molecules on Mars, suggesting the planet may have once supported life
Spirit rover discovered evidence that Mars once had water on its surface by analyzing rock formations in Gusev Crater
NASA's WISE spacecraft discovered the coldest brown dwarf star known, with a temperature close to Earth's
Kepler Space Telescope discovered over 2,600 confirmed exoplanets before its mission ended
JWST discovered the most distant known galaxy JADES-GS-z14-0, seen as it was 290 million years after the Big Bang
The SLS rocket is the most powerful rocket NASA has ever built, producing 8.8 million pounds of thrust at liftoff
Orion spacecraft can support four astronauts for up to 21 days, designed for deep space exploration
Artemis program aims to land the first woman and first person of color on the Moon
Gateway, a small space station orbiting the Moon, will serve as a staging point for Artemis lunar surface missions
The Voyager Golden Records carry sounds and images of Earth for any extraterrestrial civilization that might find them
Pioneer plaques depict a nude man and woman and a map showing Earth's location in the galaxy
Robert Goddard launched the world's first liquid-fueled rocket in Auburn, Massachusetts
Wernher von Braun led the development of the Saturn V rocket that carried astronauts to the Moon
Katherine Johnson's calculations were critical to the success of America's early space missions, including John Glenn's orbital flight
Margaret Hamilton led the team that developed the Apollo Guidance Computer software, coining the term 'software engineering'
Gene Kranz led Mission Control during Apollo 11 and Apollo 13, famous for saying 'Failure is not an option'
Chris Kraft designed NASA's Mission Control Center and flight control protocols that are still used today
STS-3 landed at White Sands, New Mexico, the only shuttle landing there due to flooding at Edwards Air Force Base
STS-4 was the last orbital test flight of the Space Shuttle before operational missions began
STS-7 deployed two communications satellites and used the shuttle's robotic arm to deploy and retrieve a test satellite
STS-8 was the first Space Shuttle mission to launch and land at night
STS-41-B was the first mission to use the untethered Manned Maneuvering Unit and deploy satellites from the orbiter bay
STS-51-B carried Spacelab 3, conducting materials science experiments in microgravity
STS-51-F suffered the only in-flight abort in Space Shuttle history when an engine shut down during ascent
STS-51-G deployed three communications satellites and carried Saudi Prince Sultan bin Salman, the first Arab in space
STS-51-I deployed three satellites and successfully retrieved, repaired, and redeployed the Syncom IV-3 satellite
STS-61-A had the largest crew to fly on a single spacecraft with 8 astronauts, carrying Spacelab D1 for Germany
STS-61-C carried Congressman Bill Nelson, who later became NASA Administrator under President Biden
STS-27 was a classified Department of Defense mission that deployed a Lacrosse radar imaging satellite
STS-29 deployed the third Tracking and Data Relay Satellite, restoring full communication coverage for NASA
STS-28 was a classified mission that deployed a KH-11 reconnaissance satellite
STS-32 retrieved the Long Duration Exposure Facility after nearly 6 years in orbit, the longest shuttle mission to that point
STS-36 was a classified Department of Defense mission that deployed a signals intelligence satellite
STS-41 deployed the Ulysses solar probe on a trajectory to study the Sun's poles
STS-35 carried the ASTRO-1 astronomy observatory, studying ultraviolet and X-ray sources
STS-39 was the first unclassified Department of Defense Space Shuttle mission
STS-40 carried the first dedicated life sciences Spacelab mission (SLS-1)
STS-43 deployed the fifth Tracking and Data Relay Satellite
STS-44 deployed the Defense Support Program early warning satellite and tested Terra Scout sensors
STS-42 carried the first International Microgravity Laboratory with experiments from 16 countries
STS-45 carried the first Atmospheric Laboratory for Applications and Science (ATLAS-1)
STS-50 carried the first United States Microgravity Laboratory for materials science research
STS-46 deployed the European Retrievable Carrier and tested the Tethered Satellite System
STS-52 deployed the Laser Geodynamics Satellite II for precise measurements of Earth's crustal movements
STS-53 was the last dedicated Department of Defense Space Shuttle mission
STS-54 deployed the sixth Tracking and Data Relay Satellite
STS-56 carried ATLAS-2 for studies of the Sun's energy output and Earth's atmosphere
STS-55 was the second German Spacelab mission (D-2) with 88 experiments in various scientific disciplines
STS-57 carried SPACEHAB-1, the first commercial pressurized module to fly on the Space Shuttle
STS-51 deployed the Advanced Communications Technology Satellite and conducted spacewalks to test equipment
STS-58 carried the second Spacelab Life Sciences mission with 14 experiments on human and animal physiology
STS-60 was the first Space Shuttle mission to include a Russian cosmonaut, Sergei Krikalev
STS-62 studied materials processing and conducted the first test of wake shield facility for ultra-pure crystals
STS-59 carried Space Radar Laboratory-1 to study Earth's environment using multiple radar systems
STS-65 carried the second International Microgravity Laboratory with over 80 experiments
STS-64 tested the Lidar In-space Technology Experiment for atmospheric research
STS-68 carried the second Space Radar Laboratory to compare seasonal changes on Earth
STS-66 carried ATLAS-3 for atmospheric research and deployed the CRISTA-SPAS satellite
STS-67 carried ASTRO-2, observing 300 targets in the ultraviolet spectrum during a 16-day mission
STS-70 deployed the seventh Tracking and Data Relay Satellite
STS-69 deployed and retrieved the Wake Shield Facility and SPARTAN satellite
STS-73 carried the second United States Microgravity Laboratory for 16 days of materials science research
STS-74 was the second Space Shuttle-Mir docking mission, delivering the Russian Docking Module
STS-72 retrieved the Japanese Space Flyer Unit after 10 months in orbit
STS-75 re-flew the Tethered Satellite System, deploying 19.7 km of tether before it broke
STS-76 was the third Shuttle-Mir docking, with astronaut Shannon Lucid beginning her record stay on Mir
STS-77 tested the Inflatable Antenna Experiment and operated SPACEHAB-4 experiments
STS-78 carried the Life and Microgravity Spacelab for the longest shuttle mission at 16 days 21 hours
STS-79 was the fourth Shuttle-Mir docking, exchanging Shannon Lucid for John Blaha
STS-80 was the longest Space Shuttle mission at 17 days 15 hours 53 minutes
STS-81 was the fifth Shuttle-Mir docking, exchanging John Blaha for Jerry Linenger
STS-82 was the second Hubble Space Telescope servicing mission, installing new instruments
STS-83 was cut short after only 4 days due to a fuel cell problem, becoming the shortest shuttle mission
STS-84 was the sixth Shuttle-Mir docking, exchanging Jerry Linenger for Michael Foale
STS-94 was a reflight of the shortened STS-83 mission with the Microgravity Science Laboratory
STS-85 deployed and retrieved the CRISTA-SPAS satellite and tested the Japanese robotic arm
STS-86 was the seventh Shuttle-Mir docking, exchanging Michael Foale for David Wolf
STS-87 deployed the SPARTAN solar observatory and carried the fourth US Microgravity Payload
STS-89 was the eighth Shuttle-Mir docking, exchanging David Wolf for Andy Thomas
STS-90 carried the Neurolab Spacelab for studies on the nervous system in microgravity
STS-91 was the ninth and final Shuttle-Mir docking, retrieving astronaut Andy Thomas
Jerry Ross from Crown Point, Indiana, flew seven Space Shuttle missions, setting a record with Franklin Chang-Díaz
Don Pettit from Silverton, Oregon, holds the record for oldest person to make a long-duration spaceflight at age 69
STS-96 was the first Space Shuttle to dock with the International Space Station
STS-103 was the third Hubble servicing mission, conducted on an emergency basis to replace failed gyroscopes
STS-99 carried the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission, mapping 80% of Earth's land surface in 3D
STS-101 was the third Space Shuttle mission to the ISS, performing maintenance and delivering supplies
STS-106 prepared the ISS for its first permanent crew, delivering supplies and conducting spacewalks
STS-97 installed the first set of US solar arrays on the International Space Station
STS-102 delivered the first part of the Multi-Purpose Logistics Module to the ISS
STS-100 delivered and installed the Canadarm2 robotic arm on the International Space Station
STS-104 delivered and installed the Quest airlock, enabling spacewalks from the US segment of the ISS
STS-105 delivered Expedition 3 crew and the Leonardo Multi-Purpose Logistics Module to the ISS
STS-108 delivered Expedition 4 crew and the Raffaello logistics module during the response to 9/11
STS-109 was the fourth Hubble servicing mission, installing the Advanced Camera for Surveys
STS-110 installed the S0 Truss, the center segment of the ISS truss structure
STS-111 delivered Expedition 5 crew and installed the Mobile Base System on the ISS
STS-112 installed the S1 Truss segment on the International Space Station
STS-113 installed the P1 Truss segment and delivered Expedition 6 crew to the ISS
STS-115 resumed ISS construction after the Columbia disaster, installing the P3/P4 truss with solar arrays
STS-116 installed the P5 truss spacer and rewired the ISS electrical system
STS-117 installed the S3/S4 truss with the second set of large solar arrays
STS-118 installed the S5 truss spacer and featured teacher-astronaut Barbara Morgan
STS-120 delivered the Harmony connecting node to the ISS, enabling additional international modules
STS-122 delivered the European Columbus laboratory module to the International Space Station
STS-123 delivered the Japanese Experiment Logistics Module and Canadian Dextre robot to the ISS
STS-124 delivered the Japanese Experiment Module pressurized section, completing the Kibo laboratory
STS-126 delivered equipment to expand the ISS crew capacity from three to six people
STS-119 delivered the S6 truss and final set of large solar arrays to complete the ISS power grid
STS-127 delivered the Japanese Exposed Facility, completing Japan's contribution to the ISS
STS-128 delivered supplies and equipment including the Materials Science Research Rack
STS-129 delivered two Express Logistics Carriers with spare parts for the ISS
STS-130 delivered the Tranquility node and Cupola observation module to the ISS
STS-131 delivered the Multi-Purpose Logistics Module Leonardo with science equipment and supplies
STS-132 delivered the Russian Mini-Research Module 1 and spare parts for the ISS
STS-133 delivered the Permanent Multipurpose Module and the Robonaut 2 humanoid robot to the ISS
STS-134 delivered the Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer, a particle physics experiment, to the ISS
Expedition 2 crew James Voss and Susan Helms conducted an 8 hour 56 minute spacewalk, the longest in history
Expedition 6 crew Ken Bowersox and Don Pettit were stranded on the ISS after Columbia, returning on Soyuz
Expedition 10 astronaut Leroy Chiao and Salizhan Sharipov maintained ISS operations with a two-person crew
Expedition 20 became the first six-person crew aboard the ISS, doubling scientific productivity
Mark Vande Hei from Falls Church, Virginia, spent 355 days in space during Expedition 64/65/66
SpaceX CRS-1 was the first operational cargo resupply mission to the ISS under NASA contract
SpaceX CRS-2 delivered supplies including frozen samples of crew member's blood and saliva for research
SpaceX CRS-7 was lost 139 seconds after launch when a strut inside the second stage failed
Falcon Heavy launched for the first time, sending Elon Musk's Tesla Roadster toward Mars orbit
Falcon Heavy's first flight featured synchronized landing of two side boosters at Cape Canaveral
SpaceX Starlink constellation began deployment with the first 60 operational satellites
SpaceX launched over 6,000 Starlink satellites, creating the world's largest satellite constellation
SpaceX completed 96 Falcon launches in 2023, breaking all records for single-year launch rates by any vehicle
SpaceX completed 134 Falcon launches in 2024, surpassing their 2023 record
SpaceX's Falcon 9 Block 5 booster B1067 flew 33 times, setting the record for most flights by a single rocket
Intuitive Machines' Odysseus lander became the first commercial spacecraft to land on the Moon
Spirit landed on Mars at 04:35 UTC, bouncing inside its airbags 28 times before coming to rest
Opportunity landed on Mars at 05:05 UTC, hitting the bullseye of a small crater named Eagle
Curiosity's sky crane landing at 05:17 UTC was called 'seven minutes of terror' by mission planners
Perseverance landed on Mars at 20:55 UTC while carrying the 1.8 kg Ingenuity helicopter
InSight landed on Mars at Elysium Planitia at 19:52 UTC, detecting over 1,300 marsquakes before mission end
Phoenix landed near Mars' north pole at 23:53 UTC, later confirming water ice just below the surface
Tony England, from Indianapolis, Indiana, flew on STS-51-F and was the first astronaut with a PhD in geophysics
Mark Brown, from Valparaiso, Indiana, flew on two Space Shuttle missions as a mission specialist
David Wolf, from Indianapolis, Indiana, spent 128 days aboard Mir and later flew to the ISS
Janice Voss, from South Bend, Indiana, flew five Space Shuttle missions, more than any other woman
Charles Walker, from Bedford, Indiana, was the first non-NASA astronaut to fly on the Space Shuttle
Karl Henize, from Cincinnati, Ohio, flew on STS-51-F at age 58, making him one of the oldest rookie astronauts
Michael Good, from Broadview Heights, Ohio, performed four spacewalks during STS-125 and STS-132
Mary Ellen Weber, from Cleveland, Ohio, flew on STS-70 and STS-101 as a mission specialist
Gregory Harbaugh, from Cleveland, Ohio, performed five spacewalks during his four Space Shuttle missions
Carl Walz, from Cleveland, Ohio, spent 196 days aboard the ISS during Expedition 4
Ron Sega, from Cleveland, Ohio, became the first person to fly on both Space Shuttle and Mir
Nancy Currie-Gregg, from Troy, Ohio, operated the robotic arm during four Space Shuttle missions
Robert Overmyer, from Westlake, Ohio, was pilot on STS-5 and commander on STS-51-B
Ken Cameron, from Cleveland, Ohio, commanded STS-74 which docked with Mir space station
Donald McMonagle, from Flint, Michigan (near Ohio border), commanded STS-66
Jack Lousma, from Ann Arbor, Michigan, spent 59 days on Skylab 3 and commanded STS-3
Brewster Shaw, from Cass City, Michigan, commanded three Space Shuttle missions
James Adamson, from Warsaw, New York (grew up in Michigan), flew on STS-28 and STS-43
Roger Chaffee, from Grand Rapids, Michigan, was killed in the Apollo 1 fire before his first spaceflight
Buzz Aldrin was born in Glen Ridge, New Jersey but his family has strong Midwest connections
The ISS travels at 7.66 km/s (17,150 mph), orbiting Earth once every 92 minutes
The ISS is the most expensive object ever built by humanity, costing over $150 billion
Astronauts on the ISS see 16 sunrises and sunsets every 24 hours due to the station's orbital speed
The ISS weighs approximately 420,000 kg (925,000 lb), about the same as 320 automobiles
The Zarya module was the first component of the ISS, launched by Russia on November 20, 1998
Falcon 9 achieved its 200th consecutive successful booster landing during a Starlink mission
SpaceX launched three rockets within 24 hours for the first time on March 4, 2024
SpaceX set the record for shortest time between two Falcon launches at 1 hour 51 minutes
Axiom-3 launched with the first Turkish astronaut Alper Gezeravcı aboard SpaceX Crew Dragon
NASA's PACE (Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, ocean Ecosystem) satellite launched to study Earth's oceans and atmosphere
SpaceX Crew-8 launched four astronauts to the ISS, marking the 50th person flown by SpaceX Crew Dragon
Falcon 9 rockets from the family have been launched over 600 times with a 99.5% success rate
The first GPS Block III satellite was launched by SpaceX in 2018, modernizing the navigation constellation
NASA's Ice, Cloud and land Elevation Satellite-2 (ICESat-2) launched to measure ice sheet changes
The Parker Solar Probe became the fastest human-made object, reaching speeds over 690,000 km/h
Parker Solar Probe flew within 6.1 million km of the Sun's surface, the closest any spacecraft has approached
Scott Kelly and cosmonaut Mikhail Kornienko completed a 340-day mission studying long-duration spaceflight effects
The Twins Study compared Scott Kelly's biology to his twin brother Mark during the year-long ISS mission
Oleg Kononenko holds the all-time record for total time in space with over 1,000 days across five missions
Gennady Padalka held the previous record for total time in space at 879 days across five missions
Anatoli Solovyev holds the record for most spacewalks by any astronaut with 16 EVAs totaling 82 hours
Michael López-Alegría holds the American record for total spacewalk time at 67 hours 40 minutes
Robert Curbeam performed four spacewalks in a single mission during STS-116, an American record
Valeri Polyakov spent 437 days continuously in space aboard Mir, the longest single spaceflight ever
SpaceX demonstrated Starship's ability to catch its Super Heavy booster with the launch tower's 'chopsticks'
SpaceX Starship completed its fifth test flight with successful booster catch and ocean splashdown
Starship's Super Heavy booster is the most powerful rocket stage ever built, generating 74 MN of thrust
NASA selected SpaceX Starship as the Human Landing System for Artemis III, the first Moon landing since Apollo
The Lunar Gateway's Power and Propulsion Element is scheduled to launch no earlier than 2025
Artemis II will carry four astronauts around the Moon, the first crewed lunar mission since Apollo 17
NASA's Roman Space Telescope is planned to launch in 2027 to study dark energy and exoplanets
The Dragonfly rotorcraft lander will launch to Saturn's moon Titan in 2028
SpaceX plans to launch uncrewed Starships to Mars in 2026 during the next Mars transfer window
Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket completed its first launch in early 2025 after years of development
